cards.definition: metals that contain iron and are magnetic — e.g. steel, cast iron. They rust when exposed to water and oxygen
metals that do not contain iron — e.g. aluminium, copper, gold. They do not rust but may corrode
a mixture of two or more metals — e.g. brass (copper + zinc), steel (iron + carbon)
a plastic that can be repeatedly softened by heating and reshaped — e.g. acrylic, PET, nylon
a plastic that sets permanently when heated and cannot be reshaped — e.g. epoxy resin, melamine
wood from deciduous (broad-leaved) trees — e.g. oak, mahogany. Generally harder and more expensive than softwood
wood from coniferous (evergreen) trees — e.g. pine, spruce. Generally cheaper and grows faster than hardwood
sheet materials made from wood fibres or veneers — e.g. MDF, plywood, chipboard
a material made from two or more different materials combined — e.g. carbon fibre reinforced polymer (CFRP), fibreglass
a material that changes its properties in response to external stimuli — e.g. shape memory alloy, thermochromic ink

questions.question: Which of the following is a ferrous metal?
questions.explanation: Steel is an alloy that contains iron, making it a ferrous metal. It is magnetic and will rust when exposed to water and oxygen.

questions.question: What is the main difference between hardwood and softwood?
options: Hardwood comes from coniferous trees and softwood from deciduous trees
Hardwood is cheaper and grows faster than softwood
Hardwood comes from deciduous trees and is generally harder and more expensive
Softwood is always darker in colour than hardwood
questions.correct_answer: Hardwood comes from deciduous trees and is generally harder and more expensive
questions.explanation: Hardwood comes from broad-leaved deciduous trees and is typically harder, denser, and more expensive than softwood, which comes from coniferous evergreen trees.

questions.question: Which material is a thermoplastic?
questions.explanation: Acrylic is a thermoplastic that can be repeatedly heated and reshaped. Epoxy resin and melamine are thermosetting plastics that cannot be reshaped once set.

questions.question: What is an alloy?
options: A pure metal element on the periodic table
A mixture of two or more metals
A material made from wood fibres
questions.correct_answer: A mixture of two or more metals
questions.explanation: An alloy is created when two or more metals are combined together. Examples include brass (copper + zinc) and steel (iron + carbon).

questions.question: Which of the following is a non-ferrous metal?
questions.explanation: Aluminium is a non-ferrous metal that does not contain iron. It does not rust but may corrode over time when exposed to certain environments.

questions.question: What is a composite material?
options: A plastic that can be repeatedly reshaped
A material made from two or more different materials combined
questions.correct_answer: A material made from two or more different materials combined
questions.explanation: A composite material combines two or more different materials to create new properties. Examples include fibreglass and carbon fibre reinforced polymer (CFRP).

questions.question: Which of these is an example of manufactured board?
questions.explanation: MDF (medium-density fibreboard) is a manufactured board made from compressed wood fibres. Oak, spruce, and mahogany are natural woods.

questions.question: What defines a smart material?
options: It is always more expensive than other materials
It changes its properties in response to external stimuli
It can only be used in electronic devices
It is a type of thermosetting plastic
questions.correct_answer: It changes its properties in response to external stimuli
questions.explanation: Smart materials change their physical properties (like colour, shape, or stiffness) when exposed to external triggers such as heat, light, or stress. Examples include thermochromic ink and shape memory alloys.

questions.question: Why is softwood generally preferred for mass production?
options: It is always stronger than hardwood
It is cheaper and grows faster than hardwood
It cannot be used for any outdoor applications
questions.correct_answer: It is cheaper and grows faster than hardwood
questions.explanation: Softwood comes from fast-growing coniferous trees and is more affordable than hardwood, making it ideal for large-scale manufacturing and cost-sensitive projects.

questions.question: What happens when a thermosetting plastic is heated?
options: It softens and can be reshaped multiple times
It sets permanently and cannot be reshaped
It melts completely and cannot be used again
It changes colour but keeps its original shape
questions.correct_answer: It sets permanently and cannot be reshaped
questions.explanation: Thermosetting plastics like epoxy resin and melamine harden permanently when heated. Once set, they cannot be softened or reshaped, unlike thermoplastics.

questions.question: Ferrous metals are magnetic and will rust when exposed to water and oxygen.
questions.explanation: Ferrous metals contain iron, which makes them magnetic and prone to rusting when in contact with moisture and oxygen.

questions.question: Brass is a non-ferrous metal.
questions.explanation: Brass is an alloy made from copper and zinc. Since it does not contain iron, it is a non-ferrous alloy that does not rust.

questions.question: Thermoplastics can be repeatedly softened by heating and reshaped.
questions.explanation: This is a defining characteristic of thermoplastics like acrylic, PET, and nylon — they can be melted and reshaped multiple times.

questions.question: Hardwood is cheaper and grows faster than softwood.
questions.explanation: Hardwood is generally more expensive and grows slower than softwood because it comes from slow-growing deciduous trees.

questions.question: Copper is a ferrous metal that will rust easily.
questions.explanation: Copper is a non-ferrous metal that does not contain iron and does not rust. It may corrode or develop a patina over time, but this is different from rusting.

questions.question: Plywood is an example of a manufactured board.
questions.explanation: Plywood is made from thin layers of wood veneers bonded together, making it a manufactured board material.

questions.question: Fibreglass is a composite material.
questions.explanation: Fibreglass combines glass fibres with a resin matrix, making it a composite material with properties different from either component alone.

questions.question: Shape memory alloy is an example of a thermosetting plastic.
questions.explanation: Shape memory alloy is a smart material made from metal, not a plastic. It is a metal alloy that changes shape in response to heat.

questions.question: Steel is a non-ferrous metal because it does not contain iron.
questions.explanation: Steel is a ferrous metal because it is an alloy containing iron and carbon. All ferrous metals contain iron.

questions.question: Thermochromic ink is an example of a smart material.
questions.explanation: Thermochromic ink changes colour in response to temperature changes, which is a characteristic property of smart materials.

questions.question: Metals that do not contain iron and do not rust but may corrode are called ________.
questions.explanation: Non-ferrous metals such as aluminium, copper, and gold do not contain iron and therefore do not rust like ferrous metals do.

questions.question: A ________ is a mixture of two or more metals, such as brass or steel.
questions.explanation: An alloy combines two or more metallic elements. Brass combines copper and zinc, while steel combines iron and carbon.

questions.question: ________ are plastics that can be repeatedly softened by heating and reshaped.
questions.explanation: Thermoplastics like acrylic, PET, and nylon have this reversible property, unlike thermosetting plastics which set permanently.

questions.question: Wood from deciduous (broad-leaved) trees is called ________ and is generally harder and more expensive than softwood.
questions.explanation: Hardwood comes from trees like oak and mahogany. It is denser and more costly than softwood from coniferous trees.

questions.question: ________ are sheet materials made from wood fibres or veneers, including examples like MDF and plywood.
questions.explanation: Manufactured boards are engineered wood products created by combining wood fibres or thin wood layers to form sheet materials.

questions.question: A ________ is a plastic that sets permanently when heated and cannot be reshaped, such as epoxy resin.
questions.explanation: Thermosetting plastics undergo an irreversible chemical change when heated, unlike thermoplastics which can be melted and reformed repeatedly.

questions.question: ________ is a non-ferrous metal commonly used in aviation and construction due to its light weight and corrosion resistance.
questions.explanation: Aluminium is a popular non-ferrous metal because it is lightweight, does not rust, and can be easily formed into various shapes.

questions.question: A ________ is made from two or more different materials combined together, such as carbon fibre reinforced polymer.
questions.explanation: Composite materials combine the properties of different substances to create materials with enhanced characteristics for specific applications.

questions.question: ________ are materials that change their properties in response to external stimuli, such as heat or light.
questions.explanation: Smart materials include shape memory alloys and thermochromic inks, which respond dynamically to environmental changes.

questions.question: Wood from coniferous (evergreen) trees, such as pine and spruce, is called ________ and is generally cheaper than hardwood.
questions.explanation: Softwood grows faster and is less dense than hardwood, making it more affordable for general construction and manufacturing purposes.

questions.question: Match each term to its correct description.

options.match: A metal containing iron that is magnetic and will rust
A plastic that sets permanently when heated and cannot be reshaped
A material made from two or more different materials combined
Wood from coniferous trees that is cheaper and grows faster than hardwood
A material that changes its properties in response to external stimuli

cards.definition: the ability of a material to withstand being pulled apart (tension) without breaking
the ability of a material to withstand being squashed (compression) without breaking
the ability of a material to resist scratching, indentation, and wear
the ability of a material to absorb impact without fracturing
the ability of a material to return to its original shape after being deformed
the ability of a material to be permanently deformed without breaking
the ability of a material to conduct heat or electricity — metals are good conductors; plastics and wood are insulators
the ability of a material to resist damage from chemical reactions with the environment — e.g. stainless steel resists rust
a manufacturing process that removes material to create a shape — e.g. cutting, drilling, turning on a lathe
a manufacturing process that shapes material without removing any — e.g. casting, forging, bending

questions.question: Which material property describes the ability to resist scratching and wear?
questions.explanation: Hardness is the resistance to scratching, indentation, and wear. The other properties relate to deformation and recovery, not surface resistance.

questions.question: A rubber band stretches when pulled and returns to its original shape. Which property does this demonstrate?
questions.explanation: Elasticity is the ability to return to original shape after deformation. Plasticity would be permanent deformation without breaking.

questions.question: Which manufacturing process removes material to shape an object?
questions.explanation: Wasting is removing material through cutting, drilling, or turning. Forming processes like casting, forging, and bending shape without removing material.

questions.question: Steel is melted and poured into a mould to cool and solidify. Which forming process is this?
questions.explanation: Casting involves pouring molten material into a mould where it cools. This is a forming process that shapes without removing material.

questions.question: Which property allows a material to be hammered or bent into a new shape permanently?
questions.explanation: Plasticity is permanent deformation without breaking. Elasticity would return to original shape, not stay permanently deformed.

questions.question: Copper is often used in electrical wiring. Why is this a good choice?
options: It is very hard and tough
questions.explanation: Copper conducts electricity well, making it ideal for wiring. Conductivity is the ability to transmit heat or electricity through a material.

questions.question: A rope can be pulled without snapping immediately. Which property does this show?
questions.explanation: Tensile strength is the ability to withstand being pulled apart without breaking. A rope must have good tensile strength to be useful.

questions.question: Stainless steel is used for kitchen cutlery because it resists rust. Which property is this?
questions.explanation: Corrosion resistance is the ability to resist chemical damage from the environment, such as rust. Stainless steel has excellent corrosion resistance.

questions.question: Which process would you use to create a hole in a metal sheet?
questions.explanation: Drilling is a wasting process that removes material to create a hole. Casting and forging are forming processes; bending changes shape without removing material.

questions.question: A ball is dropped onto a plastic material and it cracks immediately. Which property does this material lack?
questions.explanation: Toughness is the ability to absorb impact without fracturing. A material that cracks easily lacks toughness.

questions.question: Tensile strength is the ability of a material to resist being squashed.
questions.explanation: Tensile strength is resistance to being pulled apart (tension). Compressive strength is resistance to being squashed.

questions.question: Metals are good conductors of heat and electricity.
questions.explanation: Metals have excellent conductivity for both heat and electricity. This is why copper and aluminium are widely used for wiring and heating.

questions.question: Plasticity means a material will return to its original shape after being deformed.
questions.explanation: Plasticity is permanent deformation without breaking. Elasticity is the ability to return to original shape.

questions.question: Casting is an example of a forming process.
questions.explanation: Casting shapes material without removing it—molten material is poured into a mould. Forming processes do not remove material.

questions.question: Wood is a good conductor of electricity.
questions.explanation: Wood is an insulator, not a conductor. It resists the flow of electricity, which is why it is safe to use for handles on tools.

questions.question: Toughness is the same as hardness.
questions.explanation: Toughness is resistance to impact fracturing, while hardness is resistance to scratching and wear. They are different properties.

questions.question: Turning on a lathe is an example of a wasting process.
questions.explanation: Turning removes material to shape an object, making it a wasting process. Material is cut away from the rotating workpiece.

questions.question: Stainless steel has poor corrosion resistance.
questions.explanation: Stainless steel has excellent corrosion resistance and resists rust, which is why it is used for kitchen cutlery and outdoor equipment.

questions.question: Forging is a forming process that shapes material by heating and hammering.
questions.explanation: Forging shapes metal without removing material—it is heated and hammered into shape. This is a forming process.

questions.question: Compressive strength is important for materials used in bridges and buildings.
questions.explanation: Compressive strength resists squashing forces. Materials in buildings must support weight and compression, so this property is crucial.

questions.question: The ability of a material to withstand being pulled apart without breaking is called ________.
questions.explanation: Tensile strength is the resistance to tension or pulling forces.

questions.question: __________ is the ability of a material to absorb impact without fracturing.
questions.explanation: Toughness allows materials to withstand shock or impact loads without breaking.

questions.question: A manufacturing process that removes material to create a shape is called ________.
questions.explanation: Wasting includes processes like cutting, drilling, and turning that remove unwanted material.

questions.question: Plastics and wood are examples of __________ because they do not easily conduct heat or electricity.
questions.explanation: Insulators resist the flow of heat and electricity, unlike conductors such as metals.

questions.question: The ability of a material to be permanently deformed without breaking is called ________.
questions.explanation: Plasticity allows materials like clay and soft metals to be shaped and remain in that shape.

questions.question: __________ is the ability of a material to return to its original shape after being deformed.
questions.explanation: Elasticity is the property that allows rubber bands and springs to bounce back to their original form.

questions.question: Bending, casting, and forging are examples of __________ processes that shape material without removing it.
questions.explanation: Forming processes reshape material by casting, forging, or bending without wasting or removing any material.

questions.question: __________ is the ability of a material to resist scratching, indentation, and wear.
questions.explanation: Hard materials resist damage to their surface from scratching or abrasion.

questions.question: __________ is the ability of a material to withstand being squashed without breaking.
questions.explanation: Compressive strength is needed for materials that support weight and resist crushing forces.

questions.question: Stainless steel is used for cutlery because of its excellent __________ which prevents rust.
questions.explanation: Corrosion resistance protects materials from chemical reactions that cause rust and decay.

questions.question: Match each term to its correct description.

options.match: The ability of a material to conduct heat or electricity
A manufacturing process that removes material to create a shape
The ability of a material to return to its original shape after being deformed
The ability of a material to resist damage from chemical reactions with the environment
A manufacturing process that shapes material without removing any

cards.definition: the fourth industrial revolution — automation, AI, and digital technology transforming manufacturing
using computer software to create and test 2D or 3D designs before manufacturing
using computer-controlled machines to manufacture products from digital design files
building up a product layer by layer from a digital design — no material is wasted
a simulated digital environment — used in product design to test how a product looks and feels before it is made
using machines and robots to perform repetitive manufacturing tasks — increases speed and consistency
designing products to minimise environmental impact — considering materials, energy, lifespan, and disposal
an approach to production where materials are reused, repaired, or recycled rather than discarded
deliberately designing products with a limited lifespan so consumers replace them — criticised for environmental waste
a system ensuring producers in developing countries receive fair wages and safe working conditions

questions.question: What does Industry 4.0 refer to?
options: The fourth stage of human evolution
The fourth industrial revolution involving automation, AI, and digital technology in manufacturing
A factory that produces four different types of products
The fourth version of computer software
questions.correct_answer: The fourth industrial revolution involving automation, AI, and digital technology in manufacturing
questions.explanation: Industry 4.0 is the name given to the fourth industrial revolution, which combines automation, AI, and digital technologies to transform how products are manufactured.

questions.question: Which technology allows designers to create and test 2D or 3D designs on a computer before manufacturing?
questions.explanation: CAD (Computer-Aided Design) is software that lets designers create and test designs digitally before they are manufactured, saving time and money.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of additive manufacturing compared to traditional manufacturing?
options: It builds products layer by layer with minimal material waste
It is always cheaper than other methods
It can only be used for plastic products
questions.correct_answer: It builds products layer by layer with minimal material waste
questions.explanation: Additive manufacturing (3D printing) builds products layer by layer from a digital design, which means very little material is wasted compared to subtractive methods.

questions.question: What is CAM used for in manufacturing?
options: Using computer-controlled machines to manufacture products from digital design files
Storing product data in the cloud
questions.correct_answer: Using computer-controlled machines to manufacture products from digital design files
questions.explanation: CAM (Computer-Aided Manufacture) uses computer-controlled machines to automatically manufacture products, increasing speed and accuracy.

questions.question: Which technology creates a simulated digital environment to test how a product will look and feel?
questions.explanation: Virtual reality (VR) creates a simulated digital environment that allows designers to test products before they are manufactured, helping catch design problems early.

questions.question: What is the purpose of using automation and robotics in manufacturing?
options: To create jobs for more workers
To increase speed and consistency while performing repetitive tasks
To replace all human workers permanently
questions.correct_answer: To increase speed and consistency while performing repetitive tasks
questions.explanation: Automation and robotics perform repetitive manufacturing tasks faster and more consistently than humans, improving efficiency and product quality.

questions.question: What does sustainable design focus on?
options: Making the most expensive products possible
Designing products to minimise environmental impact through materials, energy, lifespan, and disposal
Making products that are impossible to recycle
questions.correct_answer: Designing products to minimise environmental impact through materials, energy, lifespan, and disposal
questions.explanation: Sustainable design considers the entire lifecycle of a product to reduce its environmental impact, from material selection to how it is disposed of.

questions.question: What is the circular economy approach?
options: A method of circular marketing strategies
Where materials are reused, repaired, or recycled rather than thrown away
A way of arranging factories in a circle
A system where only new products are sold
questions.correct_answer: Where materials are reused, repaired, or recycled rather than thrown away
questions.explanation: The circular economy aims to keep materials in use for as long as possible through reuse, repair, and recycling, rather than following the traditional 'take-make-waste' model.

questions.question: What is planned obsolescence?
options: A law that prevents product manufacturing
Deliberately designing products with a limited lifespan so consumers replace them
A method of recycling old products
A way to improve product quality
questions.correct_answer: Deliberately designing products with a limited lifespan so consumers replace them
questions.explanation: Planned obsolescence is when products are intentionally designed to have a short lifespan, forcing consumers to buy replacements frequently. This is criticised for creating environmental waste.

questions.question: What does the fair trade system ensure?
options: All products cost the same price
Producers in developing countries receive fair wages and safe working conditions
Only wealthy countries can buy products
questions.correct_answer: Producers in developing countries receive fair wages and safe working conditions
questions.explanation: Fair trade ensures that workers in developing countries are paid fairly and work in safe conditions, promoting ethical manufacturing practices.

questions.question: CAD software allows designers to test designs on a computer before manufacturing begins.
questions.explanation: CAD enables designers to create, visualise, and test designs digitally, which saves time and money before physical production.

questions.question: 3D printing is a subtractive manufacturing method that removes material from a solid block.
questions.explanation: 3D printing is additive manufacturing because it builds products layer by layer, adding material rather than removing it.

questions.question: Automation in manufacturing can increase both speed and consistency of product production.
questions.explanation: Robots and automated machines can perform repetitive tasks faster and more consistently than humans, improving efficiency and quality.

questions.question: Virtual reality can only be used for entertainment purposes and not for product design.
questions.explanation: VR is used in product design to test how products look and feel before they are manufactured, helping designers identify and fix problems early.

questions.question: Sustainable design considers the environmental impact of a product throughout its entire lifecycle.
questions.explanation: Sustainable design examines materials, energy use, product lifespan, and disposal methods to minimise environmental impact.

questions.question: The circular economy encourages throwing away products after one use.
questions.explanation: The circular economy promotes reusing, repairing, and recycling materials rather than discarding them, keeping products in use as long as possible.

questions.question: Planned obsolescence can contribute to environmental waste by encouraging frequent product replacement.
questions.explanation: Designing products to have a short lifespan forces consumers to replace them regularly, creating unnecessary waste and environmental damage.

questions.question: Fair trade systems only benefit companies and have no impact on workers' lives.
questions.explanation: Fair trade ensures producers receive fair wages and work in safe conditions, directly improving the lives of workers in developing countries.

questions.question: Industry 4.0 involves the use of automation, AI, and digital technology in manufacturing.
questions.explanation: Industry 4.0 is the fourth industrial revolution that combines these technologies to transform modern manufacturing processes.

questions.question: CAM and CAD are the same technology used for the same purpose in manufacturing.
questions.explanation: CAD is used to design products on a computer, while CAM uses computer-controlled machines to manufacture products from digital files. They work together but serve different purposes.

questions.question: ________ is a manufacturing process that builds products layer by layer from a digital design with minimal material waste.
questions.explanation: Additive manufacturing, also known as 3D printing, adds material layer by layer rather than removing it, making it very efficient with resources.

questions.question: ________ allows designers to create 2D and 3D designs on a computer before manufacturing begins.
questions.explanation: CAD (Computer-Aided Design) software enables designers to visualise and test products digitally, saving time and resources.

questions.question: ________ uses computer-controlled machines to automatically manufacture products from digital design files.
questions.explanation: CAM (Computer-Aided Manufacture) automates the manufacturing process, increasing speed, accuracy, and consistency.

questions.question: ________ is a simulated digital environment used in product design to test how a product looks and feels before it is made.
questions.explanation: Virtual reality allows designers to immerse themselves in a digital environment to test and refine product designs before manufacturing.

questions.question: ________ involves designing products to minimise environmental impact through careful consideration of materials, energy, lifespan, and disposal.
questions.explanation: Sustainable design takes a holistic approach to reduce a product's environmental footprint across its entire lifecycle.

questions.question: The ________ is an economic approach where materials are reused, repaired, or recycled rather than discarded.
questions.explanation: The circular economy aims to keep materials in continuous use, reducing waste and environmental impact.

questions.question: ________ is when products are deliberately designed with a limited lifespan to encourage consumers to replace them frequently.
questions.explanation: This practice is criticised for creating unnecessary waste and encouraging wasteful consumption patterns.

questions.question: ________ is a system that ensures producers in developing countries receive fair wages and work in safe conditions.
questions.explanation: Fair trade promotes ethical manufacturing practices and supports workers in developing countries.

questions.question: ________ and robotics are used in manufacturing to perform repetitive tasks quickly and consistently.
questions.explanation: Automation improves manufacturing efficiency and product quality by removing human error from repetitive processes.

questions.question: ________ is the fourth industrial revolution that combines automation, AI, and digital technology in manufacturing.
questions.explanation: Industry 4.0 represents the current transformation in manufacturing through advanced digital and automated technologies.

questions.question: Match each term to its correct description.

options.match: Computer software used to create and test designs before manufacturing
An economic system where materials are reused, repaired, or recycled
A simulated digital environment used to test product design
Deliberately designing products with a limited lifespan
A system ensuring fair wages and safe working conditions for producers

cards.definition: a short statement outlining what a product needs to do and the context it will be used in
a detailed list of criteria a product must meet — e.g. size, cost, materials, safety requirements
a design process involving repeated cycles of designing, testing, and improving a prototype
a working model of a product used to test ideas and identify improvements before full production
designing products to fit the human body and how people use them — maximising comfort, safety, and efficiency
the study of human body measurements — used to ensure products fit a range of users
the acceptable range of variation in a product's dimensions — essential in engineering and manufacturing
checking products against a specification at various stages of production to identify and reject faulty items
making a set quantity of a product at one time — flexible but requires retooling between batches
continuously manufacturing large quantities of identical products — efficient but inflexible

questions.question: What is a design brief?
options: A detailed list of all the measurements a product must have
A short statement outlining what a product needs to do and the context it will be used in
A working model of a product used for testing
A document that checks products meet the specification
questions.correct_answer: A short statement outlining what a product needs to do and the context it will be used in
questions.explanation: A design brief is a starting point that outlines the purpose and context of a product in simple terms. It is shorter and less detailed than a design specification.

questions.question: Which of the following is part of a design specification?
options: Size, cost, materials, and safety requirements
A list of all previous products made by the company
questions.correct_answer: Size, cost, materials, and safety requirements
questions.explanation: A design specification contains detailed criteria that a product must meet, including dimensions, budget, material choices, and safety standards.

questions.question: What is iterative design?
options: Making a product only once without any changes
A design process involving repeated cycles of designing, testing, and improving a prototype
Using the same design for every product made
questions.correct_answer: A design process involving repeated cycles of designing, testing, and improving a prototype
questions.explanation: Iterative design is about learning from testing and making improvements in cycles until the product meets the specification. Each cycle refines the design based on what was learned.

questions.question: What is the main purpose of a prototype?
options: To be sold to customers immediately
To replace the final product completely
To test ideas and identify improvements before full production
To show investors how much the company will earn
questions.correct_answer: To test ideas and identify improvements before full production
questions.explanation: A prototype is a working model that allows designers and manufacturers to test if ideas work and find problems before making the real product.

questions.question: What does ergonomics focus on?
options: The cost of materials used in products
Designing products to fit the human body and how people use them for comfort, safety, and efficiency
How to advertise products to customers
The colour and style of a product
questions.correct_answer: Designing products to fit the human body and how people use them for comfort, safety, and efficiency
questions.explanation: Ergonomics ensures products are comfortable and safe to use by considering how the human body interacts with them.

questions.question: Anthropometrics is used to:
options: Calculate the production cost of a product
Ensure products fit a range of users by studying human body measurements
Decide what colour a product should be
questions.correct_answer: Ensure products fit a range of users by studying human body measurements
questions.explanation: Anthropometrics involves measuring the human body so that products can be designed to fit different people of varying sizes and proportions.

questions.question: In manufacturing, what does tolerance mean?
options: How long workers can take for breaks
The acceptable range of variation in a product's dimensions
How many products are rejected each day
The time it takes to make one product
questions.correct_answer: The acceptable range of variation in a product's dimensions
questions.explanation: Tolerance specifies how much a dimension can vary while still being acceptable. For example, a hole might need to be 10mm ±0.5mm in diameter.

questions.question: What is quality control?
options: Training workers to be faster at their jobs
Checking products against a specification at various stages of production to identify faulty items
Decorating products to make them look better
questions.correct_answer: Checking products against a specification at various stages of production to identify faulty items
questions.explanation: Quality control involves testing and inspecting products throughout manufacturing to ensure they meet the design specification and rejecting any that don't.

questions.question: Which production method makes a set quantity of a product at one time before retooling?
questions.explanation: Batch production involves making a specific quantity of identical products, then stopping to change the machinery if a different product needs to be made.

questions.question: What is the main characteristic of mass production?
options: It is flexible and can quickly change to make different products
It continuously manufactures large quantities of identical products efficiently
It is used only for custom-made products
It requires frequent changes to the machinery
questions.correct_answer: It continuously manufactures large quantities of identical products efficiently
questions.explanation: Mass production is efficient because machinery runs continuously making the same product repeatedly, but it is not flexible if you need to change the design.

questions.question: A design brief is more detailed than a design specification.
questions.explanation: A design brief is short and outlines the basic purpose, while a design specification is detailed and lists all the criteria the product must meet.

questions.question: Iterative design means testing and improving a product through repeated cycles.
questions.explanation: Iterative design is a cyclical process where designers test, learn from failures, and improve the design multiple times before final production.

questions.question: A prototype is always the final version of a product that is sold to customers.
questions.explanation: A prototype is a test model used to identify improvements before full production. It is not the final product.

questions.question: Ergonomics ensures a product is safe and comfortable for people to use.
questions.explanation: Ergonomics considers how products fit and are used by humans, focusing on comfort, safety, and efficiency.

questions.question: Anthropometrics studies the colours and materials of products.
questions.explanation: Anthropometrics specifically studies human body measurements to ensure products fit different users, not colours or materials.

questions.question: Tolerance allows for small variations in product dimensions while still being acceptable.
questions.explanation: Tolerance defines an acceptable range of variation for measurements. No two manufactured products are exactly identical, so tolerance is essential.

questions.question: Quality control happens only at the very end of production.
questions.explanation: Quality control checks products at various stages throughout production, not just at the end, to catch problems early.

questions.question: Batch production requires retooling between different batches of products.
questions.explanation: Batch production involves stopping and changing machinery when switching from one product or batch to another.

questions.question: Mass production is very flexible and can easily change to make different products.
questions.explanation: Mass production is efficient for large quantities of identical products but is inflexible because changing the product requires stopping the entire production line.

questions.question: A design specification contains the acceptable safety requirements and materials for a product.
questions.explanation: A design specification is a detailed list of all criteria a product must meet, including safety requirements, materials, size, and cost.

questions.question: A ________ is a short statement that outlines what a product needs to do and the context it will be used in.
questions.explanation: This is the definition of a design brief, which is the starting point for any design project.

questions.question: A ________ is a detailed list of criteria a product must meet, such as size, cost, materials, and safety requirements.
questions.explanation: The design specification provides detailed requirements that the final product must achieve.

questions.question: ________ is a design process involving repeated cycles of designing, testing, and improving a prototype.
questions.explanation: This describes the iterative approach where designers continuously improve their work based on testing feedback.

questions.question: A ________ is a working model of a product used to test ideas and identify improvements before full production.
questions.explanation: Prototypes allow designers and manufacturers to test their ideas in a real working form before committing to full production.

questions.question: ________ is the design approach of fitting products to the human body and how people use them to maximize comfort and safety.
questions.explanation: Ergonomics ensures products are designed with human use and comfort in mind.

questions.question: ________ is the study of human body measurements used to ensure products fit a range of different users.
questions.explanation: This field measures human bodies so products can be designed to accommodate people of different sizes.

questions.question: In manufacturing, ________ refers to the acceptable range of variation in a product's dimensions.
questions.explanation: Tolerance allows for small unavoidable variations in manufactured products while keeping them functional and safe.

questions.question: ________ is the process of checking products against a specification at various stages of production to identify faulty items.
questions.explanation: This ensures products meet standards throughout manufacturing, not just at the end.

questions.question: ________ is a production method that makes a set quantity of a product at one time and requires retooling between batches.
questions.explanation: This method balances flexibility with efficiency by making sets of products rather than one at a time or millions continuously.

questions.question: ________ continuously manufactures large quantities of identical products in an efficient but inflexible way.
questions.explanation: This is used for high-volume products like cars or phones where the same design is made over and over.

questions.question: Match each term to its correct description.

options.match: A working model used to test ideas and identify improvements before full production
The acceptable range of variation in a product's dimensions during manufacturing
Designing products to fit the human body and maximize comfort, safety, and efficiency
Checking products against a specification at various stages to identify faulty items
Making a set quantity of a product at one time before retooling for the next batch
